title: Physics Professor — University
setting: An Australian university physics department (e.g. University of Melbourne, ANU, UNSW)
companySize: Physics department of 30–60 academic staff, 100+ postgrads
description: You are a university physics professor. Your role combines research, teaching, and service. You lead a research group, teach undergraduate and postgraduate courses, supervise PhD students, apply for grants, and contribute to departmental governance. It is intellectually rewarding but demanding — you are essentially running a small research business within the university.

schedule.activity: Arrive early, review notes for the morning lecture
Deliver a 50-minute lecture to 150 students on Maxwell's equations
schedule.note: Live demonstrations with magnets and coils when possible

schedule.activity: Office hours — help students with problem sets
schedule.note: Three students struggling with vector calculus notation

schedule.activity: Research group meeting — PhD students present their progress
schedule.note: One student has a promising result; another needs to redesign their experiment

schedule.activity: Work on a grant application for the ARC Discovery Programme
schedule.note: Requesting $800K over 3 years for a new experimental programme
Discussing curriculum reform and new equipment purchases

schedule.activity: One-on-one meeting with a PhD student — troubleshoot their data analysis
schedule.note: Supervising 4 PhD students and 2 honours students

schedule.activity: Own research time — work on a theoretical calculation for a paper
schedule.note: This is the hardest time to protect — everything else competes for it

schedule.activity: Peer-review a paper for Physical Review B
schedule.note: Reviewing 2–3 papers per month is expected service to the field

schedule.activity: Head home — but will review a student's thesis chapter after dinner
schedule.note: The work-life balance is a constant negotiation

responsibilities: Conduct original research and publish in top journals
Supervise PhD, Masters, and Honours students
Write grant applications and manage research budgets
Serve on departmental and university committees
Peer-review papers and grants for the physics community
Engage in public outreach and science communication
Attend and present at international conferences
challenges: Finding time for research amidst teaching, supervision, and administration
The competitive and uncertain nature of grant funding
Balancing the needs of many students with your own research
The slow pace of academic career progression (10+ years from PhD to professor)
Managing the emotional toll of student struggles and failures
rewards: Intellectual freedom to pursue the questions that fascinate you
Shaping the next generation of physicists
The respect and autonomy that comes with a tenured position
A lifetime of learning and discovery
careerPath: PhD → Postdoc (2–6 years) → Lecturer → Senior Lecturer → Associate Professor → Professor
